frame_label: Sindoor, Mangalsutra, Shankha-Pola, Name Change: Looking Married
generated_comment: Arre bhai, shaadi ke baad thoda sindoor aur mangalsutra pehne to aurat suhagan lagti hai, ek alag hi shobha aati hai. Aajkal ki ladkiyan bolti hain choice hai, theek hai, par hamari parampara bhi koi cheez hoti hai na, pati ki lambi umar aur ghar ki shubhata se juda hota hai yeh sab.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This is covertly misogynistic because it frames visible marital markers as what makes a married woman look proper and auspicious, implying deficiency if she does not wear them. It encodes tradition framing, husband-welfare linkage, and judgment of women who reject these markers as ignoring cultural norms.
persona_text: You are Sunita. Age 48. Married. A homemaker in Jaipur. You are at a kitty party with seven women from your neighbourhood. One woman mentions that her niece who is 29 just got engaged. Everyone congratulates her and someone says thank god, it was getting late. The conversation turns to late marriages in general and what happens to women who wait too long and what the right age is and what parents are doing wrong when their daughters reach 28 and 29 still unmarried. You are participating in this conversation and giving your opinion on what the correct window for a woman's marriage is and what closes after that window shuts.
